text.title: error-recovery Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for error-recovery as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Error codes as the entire message: "Error 403" or "ECONNREFUSED"
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Error codes carry zero actionable information for non-technical users. They increase anxiety and support tickets without helping recovery.

failure-mode.description: Generic messages: "Something went wrong" or "An error occurred"
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Tells the user nothing about what failed, why, or what to do. Erodes trust by suggesting the product does not know what happened.

failure-mode.description: Blame language: "You entered invalid data" or "Your request was malformed"
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Shifts responsibility to the user even when the error is partially system-caused. Damages trust and increases frustration. Prefer neutral phrasing: "This field needs a valid email address."

failure-mode.description: Dead-end errors: error message with only an [OK] button and no recovery path
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Forces the user to figure out recovery independently. Every error must have at minimum one recovery action — retry, go back, or contact support.

failure-mode.description: Payment-specific: "Your card is fraudulent" or "Suspicious transaction detected"
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Legally and commercially dangerous. Use neutral phrasing: "Your card was not accepted. Please try a different card or contact your bank."
